# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. watch "Breakdown" starring Kurt Russell, if you are looking for a high-octane 90 mins thriller that hooks you from the start and keeps you at the edge of your seat the whole time!!!!

**Score:** 65
**Body:** Breakdown (1997) plot- A man searches for his missing wife after his car breaks down in the middle of the desert.

* just watch it with minimal spoilers, you are gonna love this thriller... kurt russell's the best!🔥

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. If you enjoyed the ending of “Breakdown”, check out “The Vanishing” (1993), with Kiefer Sutherland and Sandra Bullock.
  - How can u skip the original and superior The Vanishing (1988)?
2. But then watch "Executive Decision".
  - Saw in the theater with friends when it came out. We were blown away by one scene. It was a fun movie 
  - taking out a certain "star" 15 min in was just an amazing decision.
    - That certainly was a huge surprise.  And wasn't it right at the tail end of his popularity?  I don't think he was known to be such a douche until a bit later. 
3. I stumbled upon this movie back in the day when I was super stoned. I was so into it I forgot I was watching a movie 😂 

12 outta 10, highly recommend 
4. Underrated thriller 
5. Relatively overlooked gem!
6. OMG my car broke down on the highway with my two kids right after that movie came out. My stress level went through the roof imagining all the terrible things that might happen...we r all safe and sound!

## 2. Pre-2000s movies that might interest an 8-year-old girl?

**Score:** 39
**Body:** It looks like my stepdaughter has a bit of a prejudice against 2D movies because they look “old”. I offered her $5 so she could watch Mulan and at the end of it she actually enjoyed the movie hehe. I’m looking for other older movies that have an engaging story and/or visuals which might appeal to an 8yo girl to help her broaden her aesthetic repertoire 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Babe
2. Seconding Casper, Matilda, and the Parent Trap

Also Pocahontas! Beautiful animation 
3. Ever After
4. Princess Bride
  - Right here, OP! This is your answer! ⬆️ 
5. A Little Princess (1995)
6. If you have successfully managed to get her on the Disney train then most of the 90s renaissance will suit (Beauty and the Beast, Aladdin, Hercules etc.)

The Parent Trap for certain

Matilda

Home Alone 1&2

Could try some Studio Ghibli

Addams Family, Casper, Hocus Pocus

Hook, Mrs Doubtfire

Clueless or 10 Things I Hate but they are teen comedies and maybe too many sex references 
7. Bill & Ted,
Dark Crystal,
Labyrinth, 
Ella Enchanted (newer but still),
Herbie
8. - Most Disney animation classics
- Pagemaster
- Ferngully the last rainforest 
- Quest for camelot
- Space jam
- A bugs life
- Antz
- Swan princess
- Thumbelina
- Once upon a forest
- Iron giant
9. Kiki's Delivery Service (1989) Along with her black cat Jiji, Kiki settles in a seaside town and starts a high-flying delivery service. Here begins her magical encounter with independence and responsibility, making lifelong friends and finding her place in the world.

My Neighbor Totoro (1988) When two girls move to the country to be near their ailing mother, they have adventures with the wondrous forest spirits who live nearby.

Robin Hood (1973) The story of the legendary British outlaw portrayed with the characters as anthropomorphic animals.

The Little Mermaid (1989) A mermaid princess makes a Faustian bargain in an attempt to become human and win a prince's love.





## 3. Movies where people are in a cult

**Score:** 38
**Body:** Movies about cults...

I'll recommend a couple, Midsommar (2019) and The Mosquito Coast, a lesser known Harrison Ford movie.

It's really fascinating how people can become indoctrinated and live in a cult, worshiping a leader who is often a completely crazy person.

I'm sure there's plenty of cult movies out there that are worth seeing. Any suggestions?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Martha Marcy May Marlene (2011) starring Elizabeth Olsen.
2. The Master 2012

Colonia 2015
3. Hot Fuzz
4. Jesus Camp
5. Rosemarys Baby
6. Hereditary 
7. The Ninth Gate 
8. Melania.
9. the wicker man 
  - The original though, not the terrible remake

## 4. Movies that lie

**Score:** 36
**Body:** I love the idea of movies that lie about how they were made. Some of the best examples are Fargo, which says it's a true story at the beginning (it's not) or Return of the Living Dead, that was like "fuck it, if we say it's based on real events it'll scare them kids to death who shouldn't watch it, but will" or Adaptation that says it was written by Charlie Kaufman and Donald Kaufman, who is a made-up alter-ego (Donald) in the movie alongside our screenwriter main character.

What do you suggest for other examples of a movie lying?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This is Spinal Tap? I don't think it ever explicitly claimed to be real but it also didn't explicitly say it was a fake documentary, either. Mockumentaries had been around but I think that was the first big notable one for the average joe, so there were folks who thought Spinal Tap was a real band.


Not the movie version but people also thought Orson Welles' War of the Worlds radio show were real news broadcasts.
2. Texas Chainsaw Massacre which is not based on a true story. 

The Fourth Kind which claims to be a reenactment of true events. 

The documentary Lessons of Darkness by Werner Herzog opens with a quote attributed to Blaise Pascal: "The collapse of the stellar universe will occur—like creation—in grandiose splendor". But Herzog actually made up the quote himself.
3. Every conjuring movie that says based on true events
4. The Blair Witch Project
  - Same vein = Cloverfield 
5. Catch Me If You Can was based on a book that claimed to be true which was later proven to be completley fabricated. 
  - I feel like it applies posthumously...like the movie wasnt trying to lie but lied because their source of truth was based on lies.
    - Walking Tall (1973), Julia (1977), and Bloodsport (1988) also fall into that category.
6. Incident at Loch Ness 

by Werner Herzog

Its a mockumentary.  Actually I don't like that word--that brings to mind Spinal Tap, Waiting for Guffman, A Mighty Wind.  All hilarious movies, but this one's more a fake suspense/horror documentary.  Werner Herzog wants to take a bunch of scientists up to Loch Ness to try to find Nessie.  So we see footage of them filming the expedition.
7. The Usual Suspects is perhaps the most extreme version of this. I think that the ending is one of the most misunderstood / misinterpreted endings of all films. But without writing an essay, the entire film is revealed to be a lie. You get to the end and find out that everything you just saw was made up.

## 5. Steamy movies for couples

**Score:** 34
**Body:** Soo I have a date coming up.. She's into steamy movies. Please suggest some good movies preferably available on free streaming platforms 😄

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Secretary
2. 9 and 1/2 weeks
  - In the 90s, my sister's 16 year old boyfriend came by to get my sister and walk to the video store. With him, he had a VHS to return. My mom caught a glimpse and it was 9 1/2 weeks. lol. My sister's story is that they returned it and rented Aladdin or something. My mom, after stewing about it for a little, completely freaked out and called her at her boyfriend's and made her come home. It was a big big fight that's seared into both our brains. My sister is 51 now and still swears they watched a Disney movie.
3. Thomas Crown Affair. The Pierce Brosnan one.
  - Rene Russo in that dress.. oh sweet lord.
4. Body Heat (1981)
5. Bound
6. Body Heat  
Wild at Heart  
Wild Things  
Bound  
Secretary
7. Blue is the Warmest Color, Stealing Beauty,, Y tu Mama Tambien
8. The Voyeurs (2021)

Eyes Wide Shut (1999)

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Rogue One is widely regarded as a great prequel with a more contained and grounded story. What do you think about it?

**Score:** 1027
**Body:** One of the best endings in any big event movie. Darth Vader’s brief appearance later elevates it to a whole different level despite his very small screen time.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It’s the best Star Wars since the original trilogy 
  - It proved that star wars is at its best when it explores the average people in the galaxy rather than just the skywalkers
    - Also why Andor was amazing 
      - It was honestly shocking it took that long to get a Star Wars spy story.
        - Truly. I admittedly teared up at the end of Andor S1 because I realized Star Wars made something good again. 

But Andor isn’t just a good Star Wars show. It’s just an all time good TV Show that happens to be in SW universe. 

          - I think that applies to Rogue One as well - it could have been in a dozen different settings and worked as well. The theme is simply "My life now to save thousands or millions later is a bargain"
2. The great thing about Rogue One is that people who loved it really loved it and people who didn’t like it never hated it enough to put effort into criticizing it. They just kinda forgot about it. 
So Rogue One was a pleasant experience because no one bothered to hate on it and the people who liked it could enjoy it peacefully!
3. Ben Mendelsohn was fantastic in this. 
  - Ben Mendelsohn is always fantastic. 
4. Rogue One was peak Star Wars until Andor.

## 2. Actress Cailee spaeny (age 27) and actor Richard Dreyfuss (at age 28)

**Score:** 320

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. If he had a fresh shave and a haircut and was wearing a suit he’d probably look pretty age appropriate
  - Honestly think he'd look more like 22-23 with a shave and a haircut. 
  - am i missing something? he looks very age appropriate to me, even with the film grain
2. Now do apples and oranges …
  - Pretty sure 27 year old apples and 28 year old oranges would look fucking disgusting 
3. If she hasn’t grown a beard by 27, it’s probably not in the cards for her.  Poor thing.
4. Now look up Paul Rudd vs. Wilford Brimley at 50
  - Quaker Oats is a hell of a drug, man
5. Hey OP

https://i.redd.it/u80562x6arxg1.gif
6. You guys are always weird as hell with these strange comparisons. 

This is Dreyfuss at 30:

https://i.pinimg.com/736x/6b/67/53/6b675307b7a67ae269eac3b06b1c0e2c.jpg

Does he not “look” 30?

## 3. I remember watching The Professional when it was first released in movie theaters in 1994, and quickly fell in love with it. Hardcore action and incredibly sad ending but there were some scenes that were kind of awkward. Gary Oldman was amazing of course. What are your thoughts on The Professional?

**Score:** 229

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Luc Besson is a creep IRL with some inappropriate desires and the Theatrical Cut of the film barely keeps it in check whereas the Director's Cut is worse, but it's an absolutely fantastic film otherwise with excellent performances by all.
  - Jean Reno stepping in to tone the creepiness down saved the movie.
  - I want to talk about this, if anyone can stand this wall of text. I haven't seen either version in many years but I remember thinking the EU release (which I think is now "Director's Cut") was a masterpiece, while the US theatrical release was, comparatively, bland. 

My interpretation was that Matilda is so desperate for a family, and so unfamiliar with what that relationship is supposed to be, that when a man in her father's age is showing care and the ability to protect her, she thinks it's romantic love she's feeling. From her miserable upbringing she thinks love has to be physical. She's a broken child learning how to be a kid and how to have an adult role model. 

Leon on the other hand, has never had anyone at all in his life, and he's learning what it's like to have a friend. Someone who depends on you, and someone you can depend on. He's regressing to a task most of us did at 6 y/o when starting daycare, but as a fully grown professional killer.

I felt some of that tension was lost in the US release, it was more like any other action movie. Good but not great. 

Am I wrong?
  
I saw this movie before internet, before knowing Luc Besson is a creep, and before knowing about the early script drafts. So yeah maybe I'm way too naive about it, and the film is all a projection of a creep's fantasies. I just don't want anyone to miss out on the absolute gut punch I got from the uncut movie. 
    - Not wrong. If you can mentally separate art from artist, it's truly a masterpiece. The US cut is bad, the tension between Leon and Mathilda (in the original) is not predatory like at all and quite believable in the context the film built.

If we dug into the lives of all artists and eradicated everything based on their lives, we'd just end up losing a lot of good art. I hope society can at least understand these can be two different things and have a conversation about it. For now.
2. Wonderfully strange film…

Just don’t, err… look too closely.

There are implications there (in the directors cut) that are…. Not…. Great….

Action is top notch though!
  - Absolutely do not read the original screenplay.
3. Great movie, but it messed up Natalie Portman because it caused creeps to sexualize her young and Luc Besson is a groomer, if not an outright pedo. 

Gary Oldman remains awesome.
4. Loved it as a kid. Kinda weird watching it as an adult. 
5. If we had a party and invited everyone who did a good job on this film I would say:     

Bring me everyone.
  - EEEEVERYONE

apparently Gary Goldman took the liberty to say that line a bit as a joke, but Besson ended up loving it. 

## 4. What's Willem Dafoe's best work?

**Score:** 169
**Body:** Been binge watching his films lately and imo The Florida Project is his best, genuinely caught me off guard

what's his best role for you?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Any goddamn scene he's in at the current moment.  
  - https://preview.redd.it/v1ck2sw6urxg1.jpeg?width=799&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=b5864c09118356bb630f6448d5baaa2b4e7e514c
2. Platoon


  - Rip Elias
3. The Lighthouse
4. ![gif](giphy|GdrxhQGjYARPi)
5. Shadow of the Vampire
  - https://i.redd.it/1e8bqnzbzoxg1.gif
6. That’s a tough choice. For me it’s probably when he played Elias in Platoon. He made an instant impression on me and I knew whatever movie he’d be cast in that he was going to stand out.
7. He’s probably one of the best actors alive.  But you gotta pick his role in life aquatic.

## 5. Favorite Nicolas Cage movie?

**Score:** 98
**Body:** Adaptation in my opinion is underrated.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Adaptation is my favorite movie on its own 
2. Raising Arizona. 
3. Lord of War
4. I loved The Unbearable Weight of Massive Talent, not just because of the meta humor similar to Adaptation, but Pedro Pascal playing against type and absolutely killing it
5. Moonstruck and Raising Arizona. 
6. Leaving Las Vegas (people are depressed by it which affirms what an incredible job Cage did)

![gif](giphy|JXuBYIHYswBkeBnYhr)


7. PIG

Possibly due to recency bias but I don’t think so. Great world building and he acts his ass off.
8. I’m gonna steal the declaration of independence 
9. Vampires Kiss

![gif](giphy|10Qm6zb5PrrVok)
10. Face/Off, The Rock and Con Air

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Watched Farewell My Concubine for the first time. A lot to unpack.

**Score:** 55
**Body:** What a film, definitely lives up to the hype.

What got me wasn't the historical sweep — though watching these characters get ground down by every political era China goes through is pretty relentless — it was how psychological the whole thing is underneath. Dieyi never really has a self that belongs to him. From the moment his mother leaves him at the opera school he gets shaped into something through repetition, punishment, and forced feminization. Being made to recite "I am by nature a girl, not a boy" until he internalizes it isn't really training; it's closer to identity erasure. And everything that follows kind of flows from that.

His obsession with Xiaolou makes more sense when you frame it that way too. Every attachment in his life ends in abandonment — his mother, his mentors, eventually Xiaolou himself during the Cultural Revolution. So the clinging isn't just romantic love, it's someone holding onto the one consistent thing that feels like home. Xiaolou is basically his entire sense of continuity.

The fate thread running through the film is subtle but it's everywhere once you notice it. Dieyi's mother was a prostitute. Xiaolou's wife is a prostitute. Dieyi spends his whole life playing a concubine who dies for her king. The opera they keep performing is literally a story about devotion ending in death. At some point you start wondering whether these characters are performing that story or just living it out, and whether there's even a difference.

One thing I kept thinking about though — and curious if others have thoughts on this — is how much the film actually commits to Dieyi's homosexuality as something real and legitimate versus treating it as a byproduct of his trauma and conditioning. Leslie Cheung himself said in interviews that the director Chen Kaige was uncomfortable with the gay themes and that expanding Juxian's role was partly to "balance" the queer elements. The novel apparently treats Dieyi's sexuality as simply who he is, but the film leaves it ambiguous enough that you could read his feelings as obsession or psychological damage rather than love. Which is a meaningful distinction.

Did Chen Kaige make a film about a gay man, or a film about a traumatized man whose trauma expresses itself through same-sex attachment? I'm not sure the film fully commits to an answer, and I think that ambiguity is both its most interesting quality and its biggest weakness depending on how you look at it.

Anyway. Worth watching if you haven't. Just a lot going on beneath the surface.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The scene where Dieyi goes into an opium induced rampage, only to be calmed by him hallucinating into thinking Juxian as his mother then sobbing in her arms, and Juxian letting go of their bad blood for the moment, and playing along and consoling him like her own child, absolutely made me break into tears. For me it was the true climax of the film, atleast from an emotional standpoint. And yes it probably plays into the theme that both Dieyi's mother and Juxian were prostitutes.

## 2. Watched Glengary Glen Ross for the 50th time

**Score:** 33
**Body:** First of all, i know it's been said many times, but what an incredible ensemble of actors. I am not an actor, but it seems that if you were to teach a course on how to make line readings interesting, this would be the movie to show. For instance, Roma says "Am I going to fix it? You're goddamn right I AM." Almost everyone would naturally inflect on the "right" as in "You're goddamn RIGHT I am." but Pacino's reading is so much more interesting. Same with Lemon reply of "yeah, I am" to Spacey's "You're trying to tell me something?" The inflection is not on the "AM". It seems though that Ed Harris is the best at properly conveying Mammet speak, his way of Dave seemingly giving an inspiring speech to George but in fact is saying nothing, he never fully completes a sentence. 

In addition, every time I watch the movie I see something different. This time i noticed that at the very end when Williamson finally puts the final dagger into Levene, and says "I think you're going away", Lemon "slumps" in exactly the same way as you he described the Nyborgs before signing the deal!

I am also convinced now that my original theory of why Moss goes on such a rant before leaving the office is because he thinks he got away with the robbery and he is setting up his exit to go work for Graff. It makes his exist seem less suspicious. The look on Levene's face as he leaves is that he is slowly realizes too, which why he adds to his chewing out of Willamson "and if you don't like it, I go across the street and work for Graff" or something like that. He was working on his exit too.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Love how repeat viewings uncover these small but meaningful nuances in performances. 👍

I watch this once a year and just noticed right at the beginning that the sign at the train station says “Sheepshead Bay”. For whatever reason I always thought this took place in Chicago. 🤷
2. Glengarry is my favourite movie of all time. Like you, ive probably watched it 50 times or thereabouts. 

I always say it's a movie where you can see the actors making the dialogue better simply by their choices in delivery. You think about the words on the page and you listen to how they're delivered and you see how great these actors are.

One that sticks out in my mind is Pacino saying "What am I going to do this month". 

There are other instances where the words on the page are confusing or just seem repetitious but the actors have done so much thinking about it that they've found a way to convey the meaning in their delivery.

There are so many aspects of this movie that are firing on all cylinders that it rewards rewatching multiple times.

I have recordings of two other productions of the play and they're good, but they're nowhere near as good as the movie. One has Richard Dreyfus in the Levine role and he's ok but he lacks Lemmon's delivery. In another one, the George and Moss roles are done by two mediocre actors and you really see the difference. 

I studied one Mamet play in university and read some of his early plays as research. His first play Duck Variations - you can see the germ of Moss/George. It's two characters feeding ducks, talking to each other and finishing each others sentences.  So that was how Mamet learned to write, doing short plays in that style, so by the time he was writing Glengarry he had a lot of practice in that specific style of writing for those characters.
  - Any other of his plays that comes close to GG?

## 3. I'm still surprise with how small Warren Beatty's filmography is

**Score:** 25
**Body:** I’ve been thinking about Warren Beatty lately and I watched some of his films, (Dick Tracy, Bulworth) and it still boggles me at how short his filmography is, only 23 films throughout his entire career and sometimes it would takes years before he did his next project and his career essentially ending in 2001 with Town & Country, a big box office bomb. (I know he did Rules Don't Apply 15 years after Town & Country, but that feels like a outlier)

Don’t get me wrong, I think Warren Beatty is a great, and I’m impressed that he can Act, Direct, Produced, and Write his own films and be successful with it, but I must admit, I feel his career is missing something and I feel he needed to have a few more films to be considered truly one of the greats or just act in a few more films. I do know that he turned down a lot of films and had a lot of unrealized projects that he wanted to do but couldn’t. I think part of why he did so little was that Warren was a perfectionist and that he needed to be involved in everything and probably didn’t trust anyone but himself in creative decisions and had to be the star. Warren Beatty is still great, but I wish he did more.

Do you wish Warren Beatty did more films?

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. >I feel he needed to have a few more films to be considered truly one of the greats or just act in a few more films

I'm sorry, you list his 80s/90s pop work as the end all be all of his career and say he didn't do much but ignore:

>Bonnie and Clyde

>Heaven Can Wait

>Reds

>Ishtar

>The Parallax View

>Shampoo


>Bugsy

and

>McCabe and Mrs Miller

?

These are all, to my mind, seminal classics, many of which he directed or at least helped in the production heavily. He's an absolute legend, demolishes every single one of these roles, but his career leaves you wanting because he didn't do "enough" movies? Would the Beatles discography have benefited from 7 additional cover albums? If you haven't seen these flicks check them out
  - I'd also go to bat for his star-making debut, Splendor in the Grass. 
  - I’m not quite sure whether Warren Beatty would rather ignore Ishtar himself. I’m also not quite sure what quantity has to do with greatness. Bonnie and Clyde alone should put him into the echelon of one of Hollywood’s greatest.
    - I know it's historically been panned but I caught ishtar on tv a few years ago and thought it was an absolute delight
      - It became kind of a punchline. I agree it’s not as bad as people made it sound. I think it’s perfectly all right, but nothing that stuck with me or made me want to re-watch it anytime soon.
        - I think the problem with Ishtar is that I think people were expecting an Epic given the title and filming in the desert and all people got was a Bing Crosby/Bob Hope type comedy
          - I think it also had a huge budget, so was a financial bomb, which exacerbates the negative coverage.
2. Bulworth still holds its weight today and is an iconoclastic masterpiece in my opinion. Nothing like it before or after to this day. I need to see more of his films. Any recommendations? 
  - His films as an actor, director, or both?
  - REDS is awesome 

## 4. In Amadeus I think mozart respected Salieri´s work.

**Score:** 11
**Body:** I have seem a lot of people saying that the film depicts Salieri as mediocre composer but seeing the film I think it´s more that Salieri think himself as mediocre just because he is comparing himself to Mozart who obviously was more talented than he was but I think there is some clues that this is just his perception and Mozart himself found Salieri a good composer. When Salieri writes his Opera we see Mozart clapping afterwards. I think this is genuine and Mozart found this Opera good. But after the emperator says that Salieri is the best composer he knows I think Mozart got angry and that´s when we see him praising Salieri but in a disingenuous way saying things that are obviously absurd. Its not like Mozart think Salieri is a bad composer but he is angry that he is a sellout and is more well know than himself while doing worse music. This is way he in another scene mocks Salieri music. Again it´s not that he hates his music but he is jealous of its success. At the end when we see Mozart composing requiem he gets happy of Salieri´s praise because he finds Salieri a good composers and seeing him validating his music is comforting because of it. It´s seeing someone he respect truly understanding his real genius. I think this dinamic is the heart of the film and i have not seem people talk about this.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’m only speaking about the film and not the real life relationship between the two composers:

The movie is about Salieri’s battle with himself and God. As he mentions, he gives up his entire life in service to God through music. He lives a celibate and monk-like lifestyle and he is okay with it because in his mind, God is rewarding him for his efforts.

Mozart is like a slap in the face to Salieri - he is rude, crass, perverted, and yet more of a genius than Salieri can ever hope to be. He gets to have it all, and it just feels so unfair. Salieri doesn’t even hate Mozart, he’s just angry at God for mocking him with Mozart’s mere existence.

Mozart doesn’t disrespect Salieri or think any less of him. The honest truth is he acknowledges him as a fellow musician, but apart from that he doesn’t think about him at all.

The movie is framed as a one-sided rivalry between Salieri and Mozart, but it’s actually a purely internal struggle that Salieri is having with his own faith.
  - Yeah that´s true. But i dont think this theme contradicts what i said.  
I will add this: It is a common motiff in the bible that God use people who seem not qualified to do what it is required. Peter denies Jesus for example and Gideon who is scared. The same way Mozart doesn´t seem qualified but he has to do God work composing for him. Salieri has to be humbled and accept this which is also I think a motiff in the bible. Maybe i am overextending but that´s what i interpreted.
2. The movie and the play it is based on are a distortion of reality. Salieri consistently championed Mozart and they were friendly. Mozart saw enemies everywhere, but his biggest target was not Salieri and his true biggest enemy was himself. Salieri was a good and progressive composer who happened to encounter a true genius. 

New Yorker 2019 article https://archive.is/jpb9l

  - And Mozart respected Salieri more than some other Italian composers. 
3. I never got the impression Mozart respected Salieris music honestly, especially since the whole movie he clearly has distain for Italian music. 

Salieri respected Mozarts music, but just not Mozart as a person. 

Salieri isn’t a hack either…. but he’s no Mozart. Their last scene together shows this.
4. I don’t think Mozart really respected Salieri’s music, but he respected Salieri (I’m just talking about the movie here). I think a lot of people misunderstand this movie on a very basic level. They see it as a man tortured by his lack of talent instead of a man ruined by his narrow minded sense of greatness. Mozart clearly needs a father figure and Salieri is positioned to be that. Mozart likes and respects Salieri and would listen to his advice. If Salieri had valued that relationship more than his own personal glory he could have contributed to Mozart producing tons more genius music. Salieri needed to see that greatness is about more than just achievement. 
5. Hard to square with this scene:  https://youtu.be/zO8FlRw5QFY?si=Y8I2_YkQDtCBoihX
(jump to 2:00 if impatient)

You'd have to rationalize saying that WAM was playing to the crowd, lying about his true feelings, etc etc. Which you could but it would take some gymnastics.
  - this is again just my perception, but i intrepeted that scene more jelousy for his success more than anything. Just the fact that he doesn´t want to play anyone else because he finds all of them boring i think helps my case.
6. My impression is that they both respect Salieri's music *for now*. But he played to the crowd, so his music will be a flash in the pan as tastes change. He mocks his music at the party not because it's bad or lacking in skill, but it's stuffy and impossible to vibe with in a place like that.

It's ultimately the reason for wanting Mozart dead and why living so long is a curse. Salieri was the only one to see that future.
7. I think Mozart just respects him as a fellow composer and contemporary. I don’t think he finds Salieri’s work all that profound. “What can one say when one hears such music, but Salieri” 

## 5. Kieslowski's Dekalog II

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I just watched Dekalog II and I understand the standard interpretation is that the Doctor has "taken the name of God in vain" by making a definite pronouncement. I disagree with that conclusion.

I think the Doctor only said the man would die so the woman wouldn't abort the child.

  
I think the person taking God's name in vain was the woman, who tried to be God by claiming authority over the life of her child.
